title: Your customers are already searching. Can they find you?
excerpt: Every day, people in Malé and Hulhumalé search Google for services like yours. If your business doesn't show up, the enquiry goes to someone who does.
body:
Open Google right now and search for your own service, "aircon repair Malé", "catering Hulhumalé", "boat charter Maldives". Who shows up?

If it isn't you, that's not bad luck. It's a missing system. Businesses that appear on Google share three things: a Google Business Profile that is complete and active, a website Google can read and trust, and reviews that keep arriving.

None of this requires a big budget. A one-page website with the right structure, connected to a verified Google Business Profile, is often enough to start appearing for local searches within weeks.

The cost of waiting is real: every week, enquiries that could have been yours go to a competitor who showed up first.

title: Stop losing enquiries in WhatsApp and Instagram DMs
excerpt: DMs are an easy place to lose enquiries. Here's how a simple enquiry system turns scattered messages into a customer list you control.
body:
Most Maldivian businesses run on DMs. It works, until it doesn't.

A customer messages on Instagram. You're busy on a job. Three days later you remember, scroll back, and they've already booked someone else. Multiply that by a year and you're looking at serious lost income.

The fix isn't more discipline. It's a system: an enquiry form on your website that captures the customer's name, number and what they need, automatically, in one list, with nothing buried under 200 other chats.

Add a simple lead dashboard and you can see every enquiry, its status, and who needs a follow-up today. That's not 'big company software'. It's a basic tool every business taking more than five enquiries a week should have.

title: Your website should answer, sell, and collect leads while you sleep
excerpt: A brochure website just sits there. A working website answers questions, takes bookings and collects enquiries 24/7, even at 2am.
body:
There are two kinds of websites.

The first is a digital brochure: nice photos, an About page, a phone number. It answers nothing, sells nothing, and depends on you being awake.

The second is a working website: it answers the questions customers actually ask (prices, availability, location, proof of quality), lets them book or enquire on the spot, and drops every lead into a list you check each morning.

The difference in build cost is small. The difference in results is not. When a guesthouse enquiry arrives at 2am from a different timezone, the brochure site loses it. The working site confirms the booking before you wake up.

Before you build (or rebuild), write down the top ten questions customers ask you on WhatsApp. If your website can't answer all ten, it isn't finished.

title: What a business dashboard actually does (in plain language)
excerpt: Forget the tech jargon. A dashboard is one screen that answers: how many enquiries this month, which jobs are pending, and where the money went.
body:
"Dashboard" sounds technical. It isn't.

A business dashboard is one screen that answers the questions you currently answer by scrolling chats and checking notebooks: How many enquiries came in this month? Which bookings are confirmed for next week? Which staff task is overdue? How much petty cash went out, and on what?

You don't need every module on day one. Most businesses start with two: a lead list (every enquiry, one place) and either bookings or tasks, depending on the business.

The test of a good dashboard is simple: can the owner check the whole business in five minutes over morning coffee? If yes, it's working. If it needs a manual, it's the wrong tool.
